text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
POST-TREATMENT RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old female with a known history of FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma of the bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, diagnosed with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis. She underwent primary debulking surgery approximately six months prior, achieving complete gross resection (R0), followed by six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5 and 6). This examination is requested for post-treatment restaging to assess for residual or recurrent disease prior to initiation of maintenance therapy. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC) confirmed. CA-125 most recently reported at 11 U/mL (normalized). Comparison made to the post-operative baseline CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast performed approximately six weeks after surgery.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT examination of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of intravenous iohexol contrast (120 mL, injected at 3 mL/sec via right antecubital vein) in the portal venous phase. Oral contrast administered. Helical acquisition with 1.25 mm collimation reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Images reviewed in soft tissue, lung, and bone windows.

COMPARISON:
Post-operative baseline CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (approximately 6 weeks post-surgery).
Pre-operative staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV and oral contrast (obtained at time of initial diagnosis, approximately 8 months prior).

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or areas of ground-glass opacity are identified. The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. There is no focal consolidation or air space opacity.

Pleura: No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal masses. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The aorta and great vessels are unremarkable in caliber. The visualized esophagus is unremarkable.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No chest wall masses or osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement. No axillary lymphadenopathy.

Bones (chest): No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified in the imaged thoracic spine, ribs, clavicles, or sternum.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No perihepatic ascites. The previously noted mild perihepatic ascites seen on the pre-operative staging examination is completely resolved. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without discrete hypodense or hypervascular nodules. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The portal vein is patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is within normal limits. No gallstones. No biliary ductal dilatation.

Spleen: Normal size (approximately 9.4 cm in craniocaudal dimension) and homogeneous enhancement. No splenic lesions. No perisplenic fluid.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in caliber and enhancement throughout the head, neck, body, and tail. No peripancreatic fluid. No pancreatic ductal dilatation.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys and Ureters: The kidneys are normal in size, position, and cortical thickness bilaterally. No hydronephrosis. No hydroureter. No perinephric fluid collections. The renal parenchyma enhances symmetrically. No renal masses identified. No renal calculi are visible on this contrast-enhanced study (note: dedicated non-contrast views were not obtained; no gross calculi seen).

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes: The aorta and inferior vena cava are normal in caliber. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The previously identified right para-aortic lymph node measuring 1.8 cm and left para-aortic lymph node noted on pre-operative imaging are no longer evident. No residual lymph node enlargement in the para-aortic, aortocaval, or mesenteric chains.

Omentum: The omentum is markedly improved compared to the pre-operative study. No omental nodularity, thickening, or mass-like density is identified. There is no omental caking. The previously extensive omental involvement has been completely resected, and no new omental implants are seen.

Peritoneum and Mesentery: No peritoneal implants, nodules, or thickening are identified. The peritoneal surfaces of the right and left paracolic gutters, the small bowel mesentery, the phrenicocolic ligaments, and the subphrenic spaces are all smooth and without discrete nodularity. The right hemidiaphragmatic peritoneal stripping site appears well-healed without residual nodularity or concerning enhancement. No mesenteric masses or infiltrative changes.

Ascites: No ascites is present. Complete resolution of the moderate-volume ascites seen at initial staging.

Bowel: The visualized small and large bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness without obstruction, intussusception, or mural irregularity. No evidence of bowel wall thickening or peritoneal seeding along the serosal surfaces.

---

PELVIS:

Post-Surgical Changes: Expected post-hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy changes are present. The uterus and bilateral adnexa are absent, consistent with prior total abdominal hysterectomy/bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Soft tissue stranding and minor architectural distortion in the pelvis are stable compared to the prior post-operative baseline CT and are consistent with evolving surgical changes.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No pathologically enlarged pelvic lymph nodes are identified. The previously visualized right external iliac node (1.6 cm, pre-operative) is no longer enlarged. No new pelvic sidewall, external iliac, internal iliac, or obturator lymph node enlargement.

Lymphocele: The right obturator fossa post-surgical lymphocele, which measured 2.1 cm on the prior post-operative baseline CT examination, has substantially decreased in size and now measures approximately 0.8 cm in its greatest dimension. No internal complexity. No surrounding inflammatory change. This is consistent with interval partial resolution of a benign post-surgical lymphocele and requires no intervention.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is adequately distended and normal in wall thickness and contour. No intraluminal filling defects. No perivesical inflammatory changes.

Pelvic Peritoneum: No peritoneal nodules or soft tissue implants along the pelvic peritoneum, cul-de-sac, or rectosigmoid serosal surfaces. The pelvic floor appears intact.

Rectum and Sigmoid Colon: Normal wall thickness and no peri-rectal adenopathy.

Bones (pelvis and lumbar spine): No lytic or blastic osseous lesions. No cortical destruction. Bone density appears appropriate for patient age.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of residual or recurrent disease. The extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis, bilateral adnexal masses, omental caking, and lymphadenopathy identified on pre-operative staging CT have been surgically resected and show no evidence of recurrence on this post-treatment examination. The peritoneal surfaces, omentum, retroperitoneum, and pelvic soft tissues are clear of any suspicious nodularity or mass.

2. No ascites, no pleural effusion, and no pulmonary nodules. Complete resolution of the moderate-volume ascites and perihepatic fluid seen at initial staging.

3. Interval decrease in size of the right obturator fossa post-surgical lymphocele, now measuring approximately 0.8 cm, consistent with expected post-operative evolution and requiring no intervention.

4. Post-surgical pelvic changes are stable and expected. No new adenopathy above or below the diaphragm.

5. All abdominal and pelvic solid organs appear within normal limits without evidence of hepatic, splenic, or other parenchymal metastatic deposits.

These findings are in keeping with a complete radiographic response to primary debulking surgery followed by platinum-based adjuvant chemotherapy. The imaging findings are concordant with the reported normalization of CA-125.

Dictated and finalized by: Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division
Final report.
